instruction: When should you use macros versus generics in Rust? Explain the tradeoffs with practical examples.
output:
<think>
Macros and generics both enable code reuse but operate at fundamentally different levels. Generics work within the type system — they're type-checked, produce clear error messages, and compose well with traits. Macros operate at the syntax level — they manipulate token trees before type checking, can generate arbitrary code patterns, and aren't constrained by the type system. Use generics when: you want type-safe polymorphism, the pattern fits trait bounds, and you want good IDE support. Use macros when: you need to generate repetitive boilerplate (multiple struct impls), you need to accept variable numbers of arguments (like vec![]), you need to create new syntax (DSLs), or the pattern can't be expressed with generics (like implementing a trait for tuples of different lengths). Macros have downsides: harder to debug, worse error messages, harder to understand, and they bypass type checking until expansion. The rule: prefer generics; reach for macros when generics can't express the pattern.
</think>

```rust
// === GENERICS: Preferred for type-safe polymorphism ===

// Generic function — type-checked, clear errors, IDE support
fn largest<T: PartialOrd>(items: &[T]) -> &T {
    let mut largest = &items[0];
    for item in &items[1..] {
        if item > largest {
            largest = item;
        }
    }
    largest
}

// Generic struct — reusable with any type
struct Pair<A, B> {
    first: A,
    second: B,
}

impl<A: std::fmt::Display, B: std::fmt::Display> Pair<A, B> {
    fn display(&self) {
        println!("({}, {})", self.first, self.second);
    }
}

// === MACROS: When generics can't express the pattern ===

// 1. Variable arguments — generics can't do this
macro_rules! hashmap {
    ($($key:expr => $val:expr),* $(,)?) => {{
        let mut map = std::collections::HashMap::new();
        $(map.insert($key, $val);)*
        map
    }};
}

let scores = hashmap! {
    "alice" => 100,
    "bob" => 85,
    "charlie" => 92,
};

// 2. Repetitive trait impls — generics can't impl for different tuple sizes
macro_rules! impl_display_tuple {
    ($($T:ident),+) => {
        impl<$($T: std::fmt::Display),+> std::fmt::Display for ($($T,)+) {
            fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter) -> std::fmt::Result {
                // This expands to self.0, self.1, etc.
                let mut first = true;
                $(
                    if !first { write!(f, ", ")?; }
                    first = false;
                    write!(f, "{}", self.${index()})?;
                )+
                Ok(())
            }
        }
    };
}

// 3. Code generation from patterns
macro_rules! define_errors {
    ($($name:ident: $msg:expr),* $(,)?) => {
        $(
            #[derive(Debug)]
            pub struct $name;

            impl std::fmt::Display for $name {
                fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter) -> std::fmt::Result {
                    write!(f, $msg)
                }
            }

            impl std::error::Error for $name {}
        )*
    };
}

define_errors! {
    NotFoundError: "resource not found",
    AuthError: "authentication required",
    TimeoutError: "operation timed out",
}

// Each generates a full struct + Display + Error impl

// === WHEN TO USE WHICH ===

// ✅ Generic: "I want to work with any type that has trait X"
fn serialize<T: serde::Serialize>(value: &T) -> String {
    serde_json::to_string(value).unwrap()
}

// ✅ Macro: "I want to generate similar code for many types"
macro_rules! impl_from_for_error {
    ($($source:ty => $variant:ident),* $(,)?) => {
        $(impl From<$source> for AppError {
            fn from(err: $source) -> Self {
                AppError::$variant(err.to_string())
            }
        })*
    };
}

// ✅ Generic: single behavior, different types
// ✅ Macro: repetitive boilerplate, DSL syntax, variable args
// ❌ Macro when generic works: harder to debug, worse errors

fn main() {
    println!("{}", largest(&[1, 5, 3, 8, 2]));  // Generic
    println!("{:?}", scores);  // Macro-created HashMap
    println!("{}", NotFoundError);  // Macro-generated error type
}
```

instruction: Show practical macro_rules! patterns in Rust. How do you handle different match arms, repetitions, and nested patterns?
output:
<think>
Rust's declarative macros (macro_rules!) use pattern matching on token trees. The key fragment specifiers are: expr (expressions), ident (identifiers), ty (types), pat (patterns), stmt (statements), block (blocks), literal (literals), and tt (single token tree — the catch-all). Repetition uses $(...)* for zero-or-more and $(...)+ for one-or-more, with optional separator tokens. Multiple match arms try patterns in order, like a match statement. Common patterns include: recursive macros for building nested structures, optional arguments with $(...)?, combining multiple fragment types, and using tt for flexible forwarding. Gotchas: macros are hygienic by default (variables defined inside don't leak out, but items like structs DO), recursion depth is limited (128 by default), and error messages from failed macro matches are notoriously bad. The key to good macro_rules is starting simple and adding complexity incrementally.
</think>

```rust
// === PATTERN 1: Multiple match arms ===

macro_rules! log {
    // Arm 1: Just a message
    ($msg:expr) => {
        println!("[LOG] {}", $msg);
    };
    // Arm 2: Message with level
    ($level:ident, $msg:expr) => {
        println!("[{}] {}", stringify!($level), $msg);
    };
    // Arm 3: Formatted message with level
    ($level:ident, $fmt:expr, $($arg:expr),*) => {
        println!(concat!("[{}] ", $fmt), stringify!($level), $($arg),*);
    };
}

// log!("simple");                    // [LOG] simple
// log!(WARN, "watch out");           // [WARN] watch out
// log!(ERROR, "failed: {}", err);    // [ERROR] failed: ...

// === PATTERN 2: Repetition with separators ===

macro_rules! csv_row {
    ($($val:expr),+ $(,)?) => {  // One or more, optional trailing comma
        {
            let mut row = String::new();
            let mut first = true;
            $(
                if !first { row.push(','); }
                first = false;
                row.push_str(&format!("{}", $val));
            )+
            row
        }
    };
}

let row = csv_row!("alice", 30, "engineer");
// "alice,30,engineer"

// === PATTERN 3: Nested repetitions ===

macro_rules! matrix {
    ($([$($val:expr),* $(,)?]),* $(,)?) => {
        vec![
            $(vec![$($val),*]),*
        ]
    };
}

let m = matrix![
    [1, 2, 3],
    [4, 5, 6],
    [7, 8, 9],
];
// Vec<Vec<i32>>

// === PATTERN 4: Builder DSL ===

macro_rules! config {
    ($name:ident { $($key:ident : $val:expr),* $(,)? }) => {
        {
            let mut builder = $name::builder();
            $(builder = builder.$key($val);)*
            builder.build()
        }
    };
}

// Usage: config!(Server { port: 8080, host: "localhost", workers: 4 })

// === PATTERN 5: Recursive macro ===

macro_rules! count {
    () => { 0usize };
    ($head:tt $($tail:tt)*) => { 1usize + count!($($tail)*) };
}

let n = count!(a b c d e);  // 5

// === PATTERN 6: Generate enum + Display ===

macro_rules! string_enum {
    ($(#[$meta:meta])* $vis:vis enum $name:ident {
        $($variant:ident = $display:expr),* $(,)?
    }) => {
        $(#[$meta])*
        $vis enum $name {
            $($variant),*
        }

        impl std::fmt::Display for $name {
            fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter) -> std::fmt::Result {
                match self {
                    $(Self::$variant => write!(f, $display)),*
                }
            }
        }

        impl $name {
            pub fn all() -> &'static [Self] {
                &[$(Self::$variant),*]
            }
        }
    };
}

string_enum! {
    #[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq)]
    pub enum Status {
        Active = "active",
        Inactive = "inactive",
        Pending = "pending",
    }
}

fn main() {
    println!("{}", Status::Active);  // "active"
    for s in Status::all() {
        println!("{:?} = {}", s, s);
    }
    println!("Row: {}", csv_row!("bob", 25, "designer",));
    println!("Matrix: {:?}", m);
    println!("Count: {}", count!(x y z));
}
```

instruction: What are procedural macros in Rust? When do you need derive macros versus declarative macros?
output:
<think>
Procedural macros are Rust functions that take a TokenStream (the macro input) and produce a TokenStream (the generated code). They run at compile time as actual Rust code, giving them full programmatic power — they can parse complex syntax, make decisions, call functions, and generate arbitrary code. There are three types: derive macros (#[derive(MyTrait)]), attribute macros (#[my_attr]), and function-like macros (my_macro!()). Derive macros are the most common — they generate trait implementations based on the struct/enum definition. The key libraries are syn (parse TokenStream into AST), quote (generate TokenStream from template), and proc-macro2 (better TokenStream API). You need procedural macros when: macro_rules can't parse the input (e.g., struct fields), you need complex logic (conditionals on types), or you need to inspect the input structure. The tradeoff: proc macros must live in a separate crate with proc-macro = true, they're harder to write and debug, but they're much more powerful.
</think>

```rust
// === WHEN TO USE WHICH ===
//
// macro_rules!:
//   - Simple pattern matching on tokens
//   - Repetitive code generation (hashmap!, vec![])
//   - DSL-like syntax
//   - Lives in same crate
//
// Derive macro (#[derive(MyTrait)]):
//   - Auto-implement traits for structs/enums
//   - Needs to inspect struct fields and types
//   - Examples: Serialize, Debug, Clone, Builder
//
// Attribute macro (#[my_attr]):
//   - Transform functions or structs
//   - Examples: #[tokio::main], #[test], #[route("/api")]
//
// Function-like proc macro:
//   - Complex parsing that macro_rules can't handle
//   - Examples: sql!(), html!(), format_args!()

// === DERIVE MACRO EXAMPLE (conceptual — must be in separate crate) ===

// In proc-macro crate:
// use proc_macro::TokenStream;
// use quote::quote;
// use syn::{parse_macro_input, DeriveInput};
//
// #[proc_macro_derive(Builder)]
// pub fn derive_builder(input: TokenStream) -> TokenStream {
//     let input = parse_macro_input!(input as DeriveInput);
//     let name = &input.ident;
//     let builder_name = format_ident!("{}Builder", name);
//
//     let fields = match &input.data {
//         syn::Data::Struct(data) => &data.fields,
//         _ => panic!("Builder only works on structs"),
//     };
//
//     let builder_fields = fields.iter().map(|f| {
//         let name = &f.ident;
//         let ty = &f.ty;
//         quote! { #name: Option<#ty> }
//     });
//
//     let setters = fields.iter().map(|f| {
//         let name = &f.ident;
//         let ty = &f.ty;
//         quote! {
//             pub fn #name(mut self, val: #ty) -> Self {
//                 self.#name = Some(val);
//                 self
//             }
//         }
//     });
//
//     let expanded = quote! {
//         pub struct #builder_name {
//             #(#builder_fields,)*
//         }
//
//         impl #builder_name {
//             #(#setters)*
//         }
//
//         impl #name {
//             pub fn builder() -> #builder_name {
//                 #builder_name { /* all None */ }
//             }
//         }
//     };
//
//     TokenStream::from(expanded)
// }

// Usage:
// #[derive(Builder)]
// struct Config {
//     host: String,
//     port: u16,
//     workers: usize,
// }
//
// let config = Config::builder()
//     .host("localhost".into())
//     .port(8080)
//     .workers(4)
//     .build()?;

// === COMPARISON: Same task with macro_rules vs derive ===

// macro_rules version — manual, repetitive, but no separate crate
macro_rules! impl_debug {
    ($name:ident { $($field:ident),* }) => {
        impl std::fmt::Debug for $name {
            fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter) -> std::fmt::Result {
                f.debug_struct(stringify!($name))
                    $(.field(stringify!($field), &self.$field))*
                    .finish()
            }
        }
    };
}

struct Point { x: f64, y: f64 }
impl_debug!(Point { x, y });
// Must manually list fields — can't inspect the struct

// derive version — auto-inspects all fields
// #[derive(Debug)] on Point would do the same thing automatically
// No manual field listing needed

fn main() {
    let p = Point { x: 1.0, y: 2.0 };
    println!("{:?}", p);  // Point { x: 1.0, y: 2.0 }
}
```

instruction: Show how to write a derive macro in Rust step by step. Build a simple #[derive(Describe)] that generates a describe() method.
output:
<think>
Building a derive macro requires understanding the proc-macro ecosystem: the proc-macro crate type, syn for parsing, and quote for code generation. The workflow is: receive TokenStream → parse into DeriveInput using syn → extract struct name, fields, types → generate implementation code using quote! → return as TokenStream. The proc-macro crate must be a separate crate with proc-macro = true in Cargo.toml. Key considerations: handle both named and unnamed (tuple) struct fields, handle enums, produce helpful error messages with syn::Error, and test the macro with compile-test crates. The quote! macro uses # interpolation for values and #(#iter)* for repetition. The beauty of derive macros is that the user just writes #[derive(Describe)] and gets a full implementation — the macro inspects the struct definition and generates appropriate code.
</think>

```rust
// === Step 1: Create proc-macro crate ===
// Cargo.toml:
// [lib]
// proc-macro = true
//
// [dependencies]
// syn = { version = "2", features = ["full"] }
// quote = "1"
// proc-macro2 = "1"

// === Step 2: Write the macro (src/lib.rs in proc-macro crate) ===

use proc_macro::TokenStream;
use quote::quote;
use syn::{parse_macro_input, DeriveInput, Data, Fields};

#[proc_macro_derive(Describe)]
pub fn derive_describe(input: TokenStream) -> TokenStream {
    // Parse the input tokens into a syntax tree
    let input = parse_macro_input!(input as DeriveInput);
    let name = &input.ident;
    let name_str = name.to_string();

    // Generate field descriptions based on struct type
    let description = match &input.data {
        Data::Struct(data) => {
            match &data.fields {
                Fields::Named(fields) => {
                    let field_descs = fields.named.iter().map(|f| {
                        let fname = f.ident.as_ref().unwrap();
                        let ftype = &f.ty;
                        let fname_str = fname.to_string();
                        let ftype_str = quote!(#ftype).to_string();
                        quote! {
                            parts.push(format!("  {}: {} = {:?}",
                                #fname_str, #ftype_str, &self.#fname));
                        }
                    });

                    quote! {
                        let mut parts = vec![format!("struct {} {{", #name_str)];
                        #(#field_descs)*
                        parts.push("}".to_string());
                        parts.join("\n")
                    }
                }
                Fields::Unnamed(fields) => {
                    let field_descs = fields.unnamed.iter().enumerate().map(|(i, f)| {
                        let index = syn::Index::from(i);
                        let ftype = &f.ty;
                        let ftype_str = quote!(#ftype).to_string();
                        quote! {
                            parts.push(format!("  {}: {} = {:?}",
                                #i, #ftype_str, &self.#index));
                        }
                    });

                    quote! {
                        let mut parts = vec![format!("struct {}(", #name_str)];
                        #(#field_descs)*
                        parts.push(")".to_string());
                        parts.join("\n")
                    }
                }
                Fields::Unit => {
                    quote! {
                        format!("struct {} (unit)", #name_str)
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        Data::Enum(_) => {
            quote! {
                format!("enum {} (variant: {:?})", #name_str, self)
            }
        }
        Data::Union(_) => {
            return syn::Error::new_spanned(
                &input, "Describe does not support unions"
            ).to_compile_error().into();
        }
    };

    // Generate the implementation
    let expanded = quote! {
        impl #name {
            pub fn describe(&self) -> String {
                #description
            }
        }
    };

    TokenStream::from(expanded)
}

// === Step 3: Use the macro ===

// In user's crate:
// use my_derive::Describe;

#[derive(Debug, Describe)]
struct Server {
    host: String,
    port: u16,
    workers: usize,
}

#[derive(Debug, Describe)]
struct Point(f64, f64);

fn main() {
    let server = Server {
        host: "localhost".into(),
        port: 8080,
        workers: 4,
    };
    println!("{}", server.describe());
    // struct Server {
    //   host: String = "localhost"
    //   port: u16 = 8080
    //   workers: usize = 4
    // }

    let p = Point(3.14, 2.72);
    println!("{}", p.describe());
    // struct Point(
    //   0: f64 = 3.14
    //   1: f64 = 2.72
    // )
}
```
